narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as a tough one—68.9% chance of not passing, which tipped the scales against admission. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your grades, zeroing in on your Year 3 decile of 1.0 as the biggest red flag, the strongest pull toward struggle because it showed challenges keeping pace with peers late in your undergrad journey. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, also a 1.0, reinforcing a pattern of being at the bottom of your class early on, making the model worry about building momentum. Your LSAT score of 25.0 added to that concern, signaling to the model a hurdle in the kind of analytical firepower the bar demands. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.6, your middle family income bracket nudged things further toward risk, as the model links that to fewer resources for the intense prep ahead. Your Asian background and being female played roles too, subtly tilting the prediction based on patterns it's seen in past students facing similar barriers. Going full-time wrapped it all together, painting a picture of a high-pressure path without much buffer. It's not that any one thing doomed you—it's this combination that made the model see echoes of students who, despite their drive, hit walls at the bar. You've got real strengths here, like that UGPA, and this isn't the end; maybe targeting LSAT prep or a program with stronger support could shift your story next time. Hang in there—you're capable of rewriting this narrative.
